moving-around-try-it-1.title: Enter the drafts folder
moving-around-try-it-1.prompt: The workspace has a `drafts` folder. Type `cd drafts`, then use `pwd` or `ls` to confirm you moved.
moving-around-try-it-1.hint: Use the terminal. Check your work with `pwd`, `ls`, `cat`, or `find` before clicking Run.

help.concept: This task checks the workspace you build, not one perfect command line.
help.hint_1: Create folders before putting files inside them.
help.hint_2: When you are unsure, inspect the folder first, then make the next small change.

try_moving_around_sketch2.title: Move through a nested path
try_moving_around_sketch2.prompt: Use a relative path to enter a nested folder.

cd-into-folder.title: Use `cd` to enter a folder
cd-into-folder.bodyMarkdown:
`cd` means change directory. A directory is a folder.

If `ls` shows a folder named `drafts`, you can enter it with:

```bash
cd drafts
```

After moving, type `pwd` or `ls` to confirm where you landed.

cd-up-one-level.title: Use `cd ..` to go up one folder
cd-up-one-level.bodyMarkdown:
The shortcut `..` means the parent folder, one level up.

```bash
cd ..
```

This is useful when you entered the wrong folder or finished checking a folder and want to go back.

relative-paths.title: Use simple relative paths
relative-paths.bodyMarkdown:
A relative path starts from where you are now. If you are in `club-hub` and there is a folder called `events/photos`, you can type:

```bash
cd events/photos
```

That moves through two folders in one command. Keep using `pwd` and `ls` to stay oriented.
